title: Solid Propellant Rocket Motors: Internal Ballistics
text:
1 Welcome
This book presents a technical deep-dive into the internal ballistics of solid propellant rocket motors — from fundamental combustion physics to practical design and simulation methods.
crumbs: 1  Solid Propellant Rocket Motors: Internal Ballistics

title: Solid Propellant Rocket Motors: Internal Ballistics
text:
1.1 What This Book Covers
Fundamentals - Propellant chemistry and combustion - Burning rate laws and scaling effects - Solid grain geometry and design principles
Core Internal Ballistics - Chamber pressure prediction - Mass generation and flow balance - Thrust curve generation - Specific impulse and performance metrics
Advanced Topics - Transient behavior and ignition - Erosive burning and stability - Numerical modeling techniques
crumbs: 1  Solid Propellant Rocket Motors: Internal Ballistics

title: Solid Propellant Rocket Motors: Internal Ballistics
text:
1.2 Who This Book Is For

Amateur and experimental rocketeers
Aerospace engineering students
Hobbyists building solid rocket motors
Anyone interested in the science behind solid propulsion
crumbs: 1  Solid Propellant Rocket Motors: Internal Ballistics

title: Solid Propellant Rocket Motors: Internal Ballistics
text:

crumbs: 1  Solid Propellant Rocket Motors: Internal Ballistics

title: 2  Introduction to Solid Rocket Internal Ballistics
text:
2.1 What is Internal Ballistics?
Solid rocket motors are widely used in missiles, launch vehicles, and amateur rocketry due to their simplicity, reliability, and high thrust-to-weight ratio.
Internal ballistics is the study of the processes occurring inside the rocket motor from ignition until burnout. It includes:
crumbs: 2  Introduction to Solid Rocket Internal Ballistics

title: 2  Introduction to Solid Rocket Internal Ballistics
text:
Ignition and flame spreading
Propellant combustion
Gas generation and pressure buildup
Nozzle flow and thrust generation
crumbs: 2  Introduction to Solid Rocket Internal Ballistics

title: 2  Introduction to Solid Rocket Internal Ballistics
text:
2.2 Why It Matters
Accurate internal ballistics modeling allows engineers to: - Predict pressure-time curves - Optimize grain geometry - Ensure structural integrity - Maximize performance and safety
This book focuses on the theoretical foundations and practical computational methods used in solid rocket motor design.
crumbs: 2  Introduction to Solid Rocket Internal Ballistics

title: 3  Solid Propellants
text:
3.1 Introduction
Solid propellants are energetic materials that contain both fuel and oxidizer in a solid matrix. They are the heart of solid rocket motors, providing the chemical energy converted into thrust.
crumbs: 3  Solid Propellants

title: 3  Solid Propellants
text:
3.2 Classification of Solid Propellants

3.2.1 Double-Base Propellants

Composed primarily of nitrocellulose and nitroglycerin
Homogeneous (uniform composition)
Common in older tactical missiles



3.2.2 Composite Propellants

Most widely used today
Heterogeneous: oxidizer crystals (usually AP — ammonium perchlorate) suspended in a fuel binder (HTPB, PBAN, etc.)
Higher performance and better mechanical properties



3.2.3 Composite Modified Double-Base (CMDB)

Hybrid of the two above
crumbs: 3  Solid Propellants

title: 3  Solid Propellants
text:
3.3 Key Ingredients

Oxidizer: Ammonium Perchlorate (AP), Ammonium Nitrate (AN)
Fuel/Binder: Hydroxyl-terminated polybutadiene (HTPB), Polybutadiene acrylonitrile (PBAN)
Metal Fuel: Aluminum powder (increases specific impulse and density)
Burn Rate Modifiers: Iron oxide, copper chromite
Plasticizers and Curatives
crumbs: 3  Solid Propellants

title: 3  Solid Propellants
text:
3.4 Important Properties

Burning rate (typically 5–25 mm/s at 70 bar)
Pressure exponent (n in Saint-Robert’s law: \(r = a P^n\))
Density
Mechanical properties (tensile strength, elongation)
Sensitivity (shock, friction, thermal)
crumbs: 3  Solid Propellants

title: 3  Solid Propellants
text:
3.5 Burning Rate Law (Vieille’s / Saint-Robert’s Law)
\[
r = a P^n
\]
Where: - \(r\) = linear burning rate (mm/s) - \(P\) = chamber pressure (bar or MPa) - \(a\) = burn rate coefficient (pressure and temperature dependent) - \(n\) = pressure exponent (usually 0.2 – 0.7)
crumbs: 3  Solid Propellants

title: 3  Solid Propellants
text:
3.6 Next Steps
In the following chapters we will explore how propellant properties, grain geometry, and nozzle design interact to determine the internal ballistics behavior of a solid rocket motor.
crumbs: 3  Solid Propellants

title: 4  Grain Design and Geometry
text:
4.1 Introduction
Grain design is one of the most critical aspects of solid rocket motor performance. The shape of the propellant grain controls how the burning surface area evolves over time, which directly determines the thrust profile of the motor.
crumbs: 4  Grain Design and Geometry

title: 4  Grain Design and Geometry
text:
4.2 Key Concepts

4.2.1 Web Thickness
The web thickness is the minimum distance the burning surface must regress before the grain is fully consumed.


4.2.2 Burning Surface Area \(A_b(t)\)
The evolution of burning surface area is the core variable in grain design:
\[
A_b(t) = f(\text{grain geometry}, \text{web distance burned})
\]
Grain geometries produce three main burning behaviors: - Neutral: Roughly constant thrust - Progressive: Thrust increases with time - Regressive: Thrust decreases with time
crumbs: 4  Grain Design and Geometry

title: 4  Grain Design and Geometry
text:
4.3 Common Grain Geometries

Cylindrical (core-burning)
Star grains
Finocyl (fin-in-cylinder)
Wagon wheel / dendrite
Conical and tapered grains
crumbs: 4  Grain Design and Geometry

title: 4  Grain Design and Geometry
text:
4.4 Important Design Parameters

Kn ratio (\(K_n = A_b / A_t\))
Volumetric loading fraction
Length-to-diameter (L/D) ratio
Sliver fraction (unburned propellant at burnout)
crumbs: 4  Grain Design and Geometry

title: 4  Grain Design and Geometry
text:
4.5 Link to Internal Ballistics
Grain geometry combined with burning rate laws determines the pressure-time curve. A well-designed grain allows engineers to tailor thrust profiles for specific missions.
The next chapter explores the detailed equations of internal ballistics.
crumbs: 4  Grain Design and Geometry

title: 5  Internal Ballistics Core
text:
5.1 What is Internal Ballistics?
Internal ballistics is the study of the physical and chemical processes occurring inside a solid rocket motor from ignition until burnout. It bridges propellant chemistry and external performance (thrust, trajectory).
The primary goal is to predict and control the pressure-time curve (\(P\)-\(t\) curve), which directly determines thrust, burn time, and total impulse.
crumbs: 5  Internal Ballistics Core

title: 5  Internal Ballistics Core
text:
5.2 Fundamental Balance Equations

5.2.1 1. Mass Conservation (Mass Generation)
The rate at which gas is generated by the burning propellant must equal the rate at which gas is expelled through the nozzle (plus any accumulation in the chamber):
\[
\frac{dm}{dt} = \dot{m}_\text{gen} - \dot{m}_\text{out}
\]
Where: - \(\dot{m}_\text{gen} = \rho_p \cdot A_b \cdot r\) (mass generation rate) - \(\rho_p\) = propellant density - \(A_b\) = burning surface area - \(r\) = linear burning rate


5.2.2 2. Energy and Pressure Relationship
Using the ideal gas law and isentropic assumptions, chamber pressure is related to the mass in the chamber:
\[
P_c = \frac{m R T_c}{V_c}
\]


5.2.3 3. Nozzle Flow (Choked Flow)
For typical rocket motors operating with choked flow at the throat:
\[
\dot{m}_\text{out} = \frac{P_c A_t}{\sqrt{T_c}} \cdot \Gamma \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}}
\]
crumbs: 5  Internal Ballistics Core

title: 5  Internal Ballistics Core
text:
5.3 The Equilibrium Pressure Concept
In steady-state operation, mass generation equals mass expulsion:
\[
\rho_p A_b r = \frac{P_c A_t C_d}{\sqrt{T_c}}
\]
This relationship leads to the famous Saint-Robert’s Law coupling with burning surface area:
\[
P_c = \left( \frac{\rho_p a A_b}{A_t C_d} \right)^{\frac{1}{1-n}}
\]
Where: - \(a, n\) = burning rate coefficients (\(r = a P^n\)) - \(C_d\) = discharge coefficient
crumbs: 5  Internal Ballistics Core

title: 5  Internal Ballistics Core
text: 5.4 Key Performance Parameters
crumbs: 5  Internal Ballistics Core

title: 6  Burning Rate, Kn Ratio, and Ballistic Performance
text: 7 Burning Rate, Kn Ratio, and Ballistic Performance
crumbs: 6  Burning Rate, Kn Ratio, and Ballistic Performance

title: 6  Burning Rate, Kn Ratio, and Ballistic Performance
text:
7.1 Learning Objectives

Understand the difference between strand burning rate and motor burning rate
Learn the Saint-Robert’s burning rate law (r = a P^n)
Define and calculate the Kn ratio (Kn = A_b / A_t)
Understand how Kn controls chamber pressure
Predict basic pressure-time and thrust-time curves
crumbs: 6  Burning Rate, Kn Ratio, and Ballistic Performance

title: 6  Burning Rate, Kn Ratio, and Ballistic Performance
text:
7.2 Burning Rate Fundamentals
The burning rate (r_b) is the speed at which the propellant surface regresses.
In strand burners (Crawford bomb), we measure burning rate under controlled pressure.
In actual motors, the burning rate is influenced by:

Chamber pressure (P_c)
Propellant initial temperature
Erosive burning (high gas flow velocity)
Dynamic burning (transient effects)


7.2.1 Saint-Robert’s Law (Vieille’s Law)
\[
r_b = a \cdot P_c^n
\]
Where: - a = burning rate coefficient - n = pressure exponent (usually 0.2 – 0.7 for stable motors) - P_c = chamber pressure
crumbs: 6  Burning Rate, Kn Ratio, and Ballistic Performance

title: 6  Burning Rate, Kn Ratio, and Ballistic Performance
text:
7.3 The Kn Ratio
The Kn ratio is one of the most important parameters in solid rocket motor design:
\[
Kn = \frac{A_b}{A_t}
\]

A_b = instantaneous burning surface area
A_t = nozzle throat area

Why it matters:
For steady-state burning, chamber pressure is approximately:
\[
P_c \approx \left( \frac{Kn \cdot a \cdot \rho_p \cdot c^*}{1} \right)^{\frac{1}{1-n}}
\]
(where c* is characteristic velocity)
Higher Kn → Higher chamber pressure → Higher burning rate → Even higher pressure (positive feedback until equilibrium).
crumbs: 6  Burning Rate, Kn Ratio, and Ballistic Performance

title: 6  Burning Rate, Kn Ratio, and Ballistic Performance
text:
7.4 Pressure-Time Relationship
The shape of the pressure-time curve is largely determined by how A_b changes with time (i.e., grain geometry):

Neutral burning: A_b roughly constant → flat pressure curve
Progressive burning: A_b increases → rising pressure
Regressive burning: A_b decreases → falling pressure
crumbs: 6  Burning Rate, Kn Ratio, and Ballistic Performance

title: 6  Burning Rate, Kn Ratio, and Ballistic Performance
text:
7.5 Simple Ballistic Model (0-D)
In the simplest model, we assume:

Uniform pressure and temperature in the chamber
Burning rate follows Saint-Robert’s law
Mass generation = mass expulsion at nozzle

This leads to the differential equation for chamber pressure.
crumbs: 6  Burning Rate, Kn Ratio, and Ballistic Performance

title: 6  Burning Rate, Kn Ratio, and Ballistic Performance
text:
7.6 Exercises

Given a = 0.005 mm/s/MPa^n, n = 0.4, calculate burning rate at 5 MPa and at 10 MPa.
If a motor has A_b = 500 cm² and A_t = 5 cm², what is Kn?
Explain why a high n value (>0.7) makes a motor unstable.
crumbs: 6  Burning Rate, Kn Ratio, and Ballistic Performance

title: 6  Burning Rate, Kn Ratio, and Ballistic Performance
text:
7.7 Key Takeaways

Burning rate is pressure-dependent (r = a P^n)
Kn ratio (A_b / A_t) is the primary design lever for controlling chamber pressure
Grain geometry controls how Kn evolves during the burn → controls pressure and thrust profile
Understanding Kn is essential before moving to full internal ballistics simulations

Next Chapter: Erosive Burning, Dynamic Burning, and Transient Effects
crumbs: 6  Burning Rate, Kn Ratio, and Ballistic Performance
